title: FC Zürich

infobox.value: or simply **FCZ**, is a professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Zurich](/wiki/Zurich "Zurich"), Switzerland. The club was founded in 1896 and has won the [Swiss Super League](/wiki/Swiss_Super_League "Swiss Super League") thirteen times and the [Swiss Cup](/wiki/Swiss_Cup "Swiss Cup") ten times. Their most recent titles are the [2022 Swiss Super League](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Swiss_Super_League "2021–22 Swiss Super League") and the [2018 Swiss Cup](/wiki/2017-18_Swiss_Cup "2017-18 Swiss Cup"). The club plays its home games at the [Letzigrund](/wiki/Letzigrund "Letzigrund"), which has a capacity of around 26,000 for league games[[1]](#cite_note-1) and which it shares with city rivals [Grasshopper Club Zurich](/wiki/Grasshopper_Club_Zurich "Grasshopper Club Zurich").

**Fussballclub Zürich**, commonly abbreviated to **FC Zürich** or simply **FCZ**, is a professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Zurich](/wiki/Zurich "Zurich"), Switzerland. The club was founded in 1896 and has won the [Swiss Super League](/wiki/Swiss_Super_League "Swiss Super League") thirteen times and the [Swiss Cup](/wiki/Swiss_Cup "Swiss Cup") ten times. Their most recent titles are the [2022 Swiss Super League](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Swiss_Super_League "2021–22 Swiss Super League") and the [2018 Swiss Cup](/wiki/2017-18_Swiss_Cup "2017-18 Swiss Cup"). The club plays its home games at the [Letzigrund](/wiki/Letzigrund "Letzigrund"), which has a capacity of around 26,000 for league games[[1]](#cite_note-1) and which it shares with city rivals [Grasshopper Club Zurich](/wiki/Grasshopper_Club_Zurich "Grasshopper Club Zurich").

FC Zürich is the only Swiss team to have reached the semi-finals of the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") more than once. This happened in 1964 and 1977 when the competition was played in its original format. FC Zürich co-founder, first captain and honorary member was [Joan Gamper](/wiki/Joan_Gamper "Joan Gamper"), who grew up in Zurich and later moved to [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), founding [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") there in 1899. Since the 1960s, FC Zürich plays in all-white, which is one of the city's colours.

The women's club, [FC Zürich Frauen](/wiki/FC_Z%C3%BCrich_Frauen "FC Zürich Frauen"), are competing in the [Swiss Women's Super League](/wiki/Swiss_Women%27s_Super_League "Swiss Women's Super League"). They are the most successful club in Switzerland with 22 championship titles and multiple [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Champions_League "UEFA Women's Champions League") participations. The team's roots originate from the first Swiss women's football club, DFC Zürich.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1896–1924

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1896–1924")]

The club was founded in summer 1896 by former members of the two local clubs: FC Turicum and FC Excelsior. Later, the official founding date was set at 1 August 1896. One of the founding members was the later [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") founder [Joan Gamper](/wiki/Joan_Gamper "Joan Gamper"), who coached and played for FC Excelsior and its successor from 1894 to 1897.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The new club played its first game on 30 August 1896 on Velorennbahn Hardau in Zurich against [St. Gallen](/wiki/FC_St._Gallen "FC St. Gallen"), which resulted in a 3–3 draw.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 1898, FC Excelsior merged with FC Zürich, and local club FC Victoria joined shortly thereafter.

The debut game was in 1896 with the colours blue and white.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The colours were changed to red and white because rivals [Grasshopper Club Zurich](/wiki/Grasshopper_Club_Zurich "Grasshopper Club Zurich") had the same colours. When Grasshoppers temporarily retired from the championship in [1909](/wiki/1909%E2%80%9310_Swiss_Serie_A "1909–10 Swiss Serie A"), FCZ returned to the colors blue and white, which they continue to use.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Zürich won its first title in the [Swiss Serie A](/wiki/Swiss_Super_League "Swiss Super League") in [1901–02](/wiki/1901%E2%80%9302_Swiss_Serie_A "1901–02 Swiss Serie A"), but did not win it again until [1923–24](/wiki/1923%E2%80%9324_Swiss_Serie_A "1923–24 Swiss Serie A").

Until the 1930s, the club's sporting remit included [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics") and [handball](/wiki/Team_handball "Team handball"), but football would become the focus of the club.

### 1925–1960

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1925–1960")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Zurich_Performance_Graph.png/330px-Zurich_Performance_Graph.png)](/wiki/File:Zurich_Performance_Graph.png)

Chart of FC Zürich table positions in the Swiss football league system

Zürich struggled to overcome an unsuccessful record and was described as the "wilderness years" from 1925 to 1960.[[7]](#cite_note-7) They were relegated in [1933–34](/wiki/1933%E2%80%9334_Nationalliga "1933–34 Nationalliga"), playing in the [1. Liga](/wiki/Swiss_Challenge_League "Swiss Challenge League") until the 1941 season. In 1940–41, they returned to the [Nationalliga](/wiki/Swiss_Super_League "Swiss Super League"), where they stayed until their relegation in [1945–46](/wiki/1945%E2%80%9346_Nationalliga_A "1945–46 Nationalliga A"). They were back in the Nationalliga A in [1947–48](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_Nationalliga_A "1947–48 Nationalliga A") and stayed in the top flight until relegated in [1956–57](/wiki/1956%E2%80%9357_Nationalliga_A "1956–57 Nationalliga A"). They were promoted from the [Nationalliga B](/wiki/Swiss_Challenge_League "Swiss Challenge League") to contest the [1958–59 Nationalliga A](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_Nationalliga_A "1958–59 Nationalliga A"), finishing in third place.

### 1960–1981

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1960–1981")]

This period was known as the "[Golden Years](/wiki/Golden_Age_(metaphor) "Golden Age (metaphor)")" by the FCZ faithful. At this time, the club was run by the president Edwin Nägeli and had players such as [Köbi Kuhn](/wiki/K%C3%B6bi_Kuhn "Köbi Kuhn"), [Fritz Künzli](/wiki/Fritz_K%C3%BCnzli "Fritz Künzli"), [Ilija Katić](/wiki/Ilija_Kati%C4%87 "Ilija Katić"), [René Botteron](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Botteron "René Botteron") and many more. Zürich won seven [championships](/wiki/Swiss_Super_League "Swiss Super League") in the years [1963](/wiki/1962%E2%80%9363_Nationalliga_A "1962–63 Nationalliga A"), [1966](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_Nationalliga_A "1965–66 Nationalliga A"), [1968](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_Nationalliga_A "1967–68 Nationalliga A"), [1974](/wiki/1973%E2%80%9374_Nationalliga_A "1973–74 Nationalliga A"), [1975](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Nationalliga_A "1974–75 Nationalliga A"), [1976](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_Nationalliga_A "1975–76 Nationalliga A") and [1981](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_Nationalliga_A "1980–81 Nationalliga A"). They also won the [Swiss Cup](/wiki/Swiss_Cup "Swiss Cup") five times: in 1966, 1970, 1972, 1973 and 1976. FCZ also had much success internationally in reaching the semi-finals of the [European Cup 1963–64](/wiki/European_Cup_1963%E2%80%9364 "European Cup 1963–64"), before losing to [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F.") and also reaching the semi-finals in the [European Cup 1976–77](/wiki/European_Cup_1976%E2%80%9377 "European Cup 1976–77"), where they lost to [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.").

### 1981–2005

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1981–2005")]

Following the club's [league title in 1981](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_Nationalliga_A "1980–81 Nationalliga A"), the club went into a decline, and in [1988](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_Nationalliga_A "1987–88 Nationalliga A") they were relegated to the [Nationalliga B](/wiki/Swiss_Challenge_League "Swiss Challenge League"). Zürich returned to the top league in 1990. The club did make it to last 16 of the [UEFA Cup 1998–99](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_1998%E2%80%9399 "UEFA Cup 1998–99"), but were beaten by [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma"). The club won the Swiss Cup in [2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Swiss_Cup "1999–2000 Swiss Cup"), beating [Lausanne](/wiki/FC_Lausanne-Sport "FC Lausanne-Sport") in the final. They won it again in [2005](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Swiss_Cup "2004–05 Swiss Cup"), beating [Luzern](/wiki/FC_Luzern "FC Luzern").

On 13 May 2006, FCZ ended their 25-year effort to win Super League with a goal in the 93rd minute by [Iulian Filipescu](/wiki/Iulian_Filipescu "Iulian Filipescu") against [Basel](/wiki/FC_Basel "FC Basel"). The goal gave FCZ a 2–1 victory based on [goal difference](/wiki/Goal_difference "Goal difference"). They retained the title in [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Swiss_Super_League "2006–07 Swiss Super League").

In 2008, the local women's team, FFC Zürich Seebach, was combined with FC Zürich and played in the [Swiss national league](/wiki/Nationalliga_A_(women%27s_football) "Nationalliga A (women's football)") under the name [FC Zürich Frauen](/wiki/FC_Z%C3%BCrich_Frauen "FC Zürich Frauen").

In the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Swiss_Super_League "2007–08 Swiss Super League"), FCZ finished in third place. In a [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Swiss_Super_League "2008–09 Swiss Super League") match, they edged past [Young Boys](/wiki/BSC_Young_Boys "BSC Young Boys") to win the league title. In 2009, they made their debut play in the group stage of the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_UEFA_Champions_League "2009–10 UEFA Champions League"). In the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Swiss_Super_League "2010–11 Swiss Super League"), FCZ finished second. The following seasons, they finished mostly in mid-table positions. FCZ won the [Swiss Cup 2014](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Swiss_Cup "2013–14 Swiss Cup") in extra time against Basel 2–0.

In the [2015–16 season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Swiss_Super_League "2015–16 Swiss Super League"), the club finished last, one point behind [Lugano](/wiki/FC_Lugano "FC Lugano"), and was relegated to the [Swiss Challenge League](/wiki/Swiss_Challenge_League "Swiss Challenge League"). Four days after the final game of the season, FCZ won the [Swiss Cup 2016](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Swiss_Cup "2015–16 Swiss Cup"), beating Lugano 1–0.

### Recent years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Recent years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Letzigrund_2024.jpg/250px-Letzigrund_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Letzigrund_2024.jpg)

FC Zürich fans at the Letzigrund in 2024

In the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Swiss_Challenge_League "2016–17 Swiss Challenge League"), FC Zürich won the [Challenge League](/wiki/Swiss_Challenge_League "Swiss Challenge League") ahead of [Neuchâtel Xamax](/wiki/Neuch%C3%A2tel_Xamax "Neuchâtel Xamax"), and returned after one year to the [Super League](/wiki/Swiss_Super_League "Swiss Super League"). In the [2017–2018 season](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Swiss_Super_League "2017–18 Swiss Super League") they finished fourth. On 27 May 2018, they won the [Swiss Cup](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Swiss_Cup "2017–18 Swiss Cup") for the tenth time, beating Young Boys 2–1.

In the [2021–2022 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Swiss_Super_League "2021–22 Swiss Super League"), FC Zürich won the Super League again after an interruption of thirteen years. The club secured its thirteenth league title with a 2–0 away win over [Basel](/wiki/FC_Basel "FC Basel"), who finished second, with five rounds to go. Despite this success, coach [André Breitenreiter](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Breitenreiter "André Breitenreiter") departed the club to join [Bundesliga](/wiki/Bundesliga "Bundesliga") side [TSG Hoffenheim](/wiki/TSG_1899_Hoffenheim "TSG 1899 Hoffenheim").[[11]](#cite_note-11) On 8 June 2022, former Austrian national coach [Franco Foda](/wiki/Franco_Foda "Franco Foda") was announced as the coach for the upcoming season.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Despite being able to guide Zürich into the [Europa League](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Europa_League "2022–23 Europa League") group stages, the [domestic](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Swiss_Super_League "2022–23 Swiss Super League") campaign saw the side gain only two points out of a possible 24 in their title defence. The league form, combined with a shock cup defeat to Challenge League side [Lausanne](/wiki/FC_Lausanne-Sport "FC Lausanne-Sport") on 18 September, proved the final straw and Foda was sacked on 21 September.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 2024, [Ricardo Moniz](/wiki/Ricardo_Moniz "Ricardo Moniz") was appointed head coach on a two-year contract.[[14]](#cite_note-14) He was dismissed on 27 May 2025.[[15]](#cite_note-monizout-15) He was replaced four days later by [Mitchell van der Gaag](/wiki/Mitchell_van_der_Gaag "Mitchell van der Gaag").[[16]](#cite_note-mvdg-16) Van der Gaag was sacked on 23 October, with club president Ancillo Canepa stating that he felt the team had "become stagnant".[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[Dennis Hediger](/wiki/Dennis_Hediger "Dennis Hediger") was named head coach on 1 January 2026, having led the team in the interim since van der Gaag's departure.

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Letzigrund_und_Umgebung.jpg/250px-Letzigrund_und_Umgebung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Letzigrund_und_Umgebung.jpg)

[Letzigrund](/wiki/Letzigrund "Letzigrund")

Grasshoppers, also from Zürich, and [Basel](/wiki/FC_Basel "FC Basel") are the main rivals of FCZ. Due to the intense rivalry, these matches are so-called high-risk fixtures, with an increased [police](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Switzerland "Law enforcement in Switzerland") presence in and around the stadium.

### Zürich

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Zürich")]

Main article: [Zurich derby](/wiki/Zurich_derby "Zurich derby")

Since its inception, FCZ has always had a fiery relationship with neighbouring club Grasshopper over sporting supremacy in the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] To date, 251 official derbies have been held, with Grasshoppers leading with 121 wins to FC Zurich's 90, leaving 39 draws; however, since the reformation of the [Swiss Super League](/wiki/Swiss_Super_League "Swiss Super League") in 2003, FCZ has frequently got the better of their city rivals, winning 33 out of 68 games (GC won 20 and 15 draws).

The [October 2011 derby](/wiki/2011_Zurich_hooligan_incident "2011 Zurich hooligan incident") at Letzigrund was abandoned by the referee after rioting by FC Zürich fans. Earlier in the day, Grasshopper Club Zurich fans stole FC Zürich fan banners and displayed them with a message mocking FC Zürich.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Final vs. Basel, 13 May 2006

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Final vs. Basel, 13 May 2006")]

Main article: [2006 Basel hooligan incident](/wiki/2006_Basel_hooligan_incident "2006 Basel hooligan incident")

Before the last round of the 2005–06 Swiss Super League, Zürich were three points behind Basel in the league table. The last game of the season was contested by these two clubs vying for the league title at [St. Jakob Park](/wiki/St._Jakob_Park "St. Jakob Park"), [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel"). [Alhassane Keita](/wiki/Alhassane_Keita "Alhassane Keita") scored the match first goal, for Zürich. In the second half, [Mladen Petrić](/wiki/Mladen_Petri%C4%87 "Mladen Petrić") equalised. Basel were seconds away from the title when, in the 93rd minute, [Florian Stahel](/wiki/Florian_Stahel "Florian Stahel") passed the ball to [Iulian Filipescu](/wiki/Iulian_Filipescu "Iulian Filipescu"), who scored. Zürich's success at 2–1 was attributed to their superior [goal difference](/wiki/Goal_difference "Goal difference"). Following the final whistle, Basel supporters stormed the pitch and attacked players on both teams.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Z%C3%BCrich&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

In 2010, the youth and women's teams of the club moved their homebase to the Heerenschürli sport park, in the [Hirzenbach](/wiki/Hirzenbach "Hirzenbach") quarter of the city, where the academy and women's teams play also their home matches. In June 2022, the club moved with the opening of a newly built "House of FCZ" also their Super League team and offices there in order to have the whole organisation under one roof.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
